title: Global oil oversupply looming
summary:
Main
With OPEC oil flows via the Strait of Hormuz gradually picking up and record-high non-OPEC exports, a global oil oversupply is looming. By Xavier Tang
Oil prices have recently fallen sharply, with the front-month ICE Brent and NYMEX WTI contracts trading at $71/bl and $69/bl as of 3rd July. The weakness in oil prices began as soon as US announced that a peace deal was imminent, prompting a sell-off in oil futures.

children.title: Main
children.summary:
With OPEC oil flows via the Strait of Hormuz gradually picking up and record-high non-OPEC exports, a global oil oversupply is looming. By Xavier Tang
Oil prices have recently fallen sharply, with the front-month ICE Brent and NYMEX WTI contracts trading at $71/bl and $69/bl as of 3rd July.

children.title: Non-OPEC exports hit record highs
children.summary: Facilitated by the US/Israel-Iran conflict, non-OPEC producers have ramped up their crude/condensate exports over the past four months, hitting a record high of 28.5 mbd in June, 6.2 mbd above the pre-COVID average (2016-2019). This surge in exports was driven by record US SPR releases (amounting to 133mb across April and June) and Brazilian oil production, as well as increased exports from Guyana, Russia, Kazakhstan, UAE, Canada and Norway.

children.title: Demand recovery is highly dependent on China
section_path: Demand recovery is highly dependent on China
children.section_path_text: Demand recovery is highly dependent on China
children.summary: China has borne the brunt of the crude supply shortage over the past three months, with crude/condensate imports falling for the fourth consecutive month since March. In June, China's imports fell into a 4.4 mbd deficit relative to the 2024 average, a period when China's oil supply/demand balance was relatively stable and stockpiling was limited.
